coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent PEG based comacromers as excitation dependent fluorophores for biomedical applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Vijayan, Vineeth M.
Komeri, Remya
Victor, Sunita P.
Muthu, Jayabalan
coredata.dc:description: We report a novel multi-modal biodegradable photoluminescent comacromer [poly(propylene fumarate)-PEG-glycine] (PLM) having excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDF) for biomedical applications. The photoluminescence of the synthesized PLM in aqueous and solid state condition, fluorescence life time and photo stability were evaluated. Hydrogels and nanogels were prepared from the PLM by cross linking with acrylic acid. Nanogels exhibited spherical morphology with a particle size of 100nm as evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In vitro cytotoxic and hemolytic studies revealed cytocompatibility. Furthermore, cellular imaging of nanogels on L929 fibroblast and Hela cell lines revealed EDF characteristics. We hypothesize that the EDF characteristics of the synthesized PLM may be attributed to the presence of n–π* interactions of the hydroxyl oxygen atoms of PEG with carbonyl groups of the ester linkages. Taken together, our results indicate that the synthesized PEG-based comacromer can serve as biocompatible fluorophores for various biomedical applications. More importantly, the facile way of synthesizing fluorescent polymers based on PEG with EDF characteristics demonstrated in this work can pave the way for developing more novel biocompatible fluorophores with wide range of biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEG based comacromers
Excitation dependent fluorophores
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental
Materials
Synthesis of photoluminescent PPF-PEG-glycine comacromer (PLM)
Synthesis of comacromer analogues
Preparation of hydrogel and nanogel from PLM
Determination of photoluminescent properties of comacromer and nanogel
Studies on degradation of the PLM-based hydrogel (PLM-HG)
Evaluation of cytotoxicity of PLM comacromer
Hemolysis test
Evaluation of cellular uptake of the PLM-NG
Statistical analysis
Results and discussion
Preparation and characterisation of photoluminescent comacromer (PLM)
Analysis of photoluminescent behaviour of the PLM comacromer
Investigation on mechanism of EDF behaviour of PLM comacromer
EDF behavior of PLM based hydrogel and nanogel (PLM-HG and PLM-NG)
Evaluation of cytocompatibility and hemolysis of PLM and PLM-NG
Evaluation of cellular imaging of PLM-NG
Conclusions
Acknowledgments
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Director and Head, BMT Wing, SCTIMST for providing the facilities to carry out this work. The authors acknowledge the help provided by Dr. Shivaram Selvam, Mr. G.T. Finosh, Dr. George Thomas and Dr. Lekshmi Varma during the studies. V.M.V acknowledges the financial support from SCTIMST for Junior Research Fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of photoluminescent PLM comacromer and its crosslinking with acrylic acid.
FTIR spectra of CT-PPF (a), FT-IR spectra of PLM comacromer (b).
1H NMR spectra of PLM comacromer (a), 13C NMR spectra of PLM comacromer (b).

Photoluminescence properties of PLM comacromer. Photoluminescence spectra of PLM comacromer under aqueous conditions at different excitations on visible region (380–540nm) (a), photo stability graph of PLM (λ ext: 420nm and λ emiss: 525nm) (b).
Photoluminescence life time of PLM comacromer (λext: 440nm & λemiss: 540nm) (a), excitation & emission spectra of PLM comacromer exhibiting large stokes shift (b).

Photoluminescence of solid micro particles of PLM. Transmission electron microscopic image of micro particles of PLM (a), Fluorescent solid micro particles Of PLM when viewed through green and red filters of fluorescent microscope (b), solid state photoluminescence spectra of PLM microparticles (c).

Photoluminescence properties of analogues of PLM comacromer. Photoluminescence spectra of PEG-G at different excitations on visible region (380–500nm) (a), photoluminescence spectra of PEG-P (λ ext: 350nm) (b), UV–vis absorption spectra of PEG-G, PEG-P and PLM (c), photoluminescence spectra of PEG-M at different excitations on visible region (380–500nm) (d).
Solid state photoluminescence properties of PLM-HG hydrogel. Appearance of PLM-HG hydrogel under normal light and Gel doc (a), solid state photoluminescence spectra of PLM-HG (b).
Morphology and optical image of PLM-NG nanogel. Transmission electron microscopy image (a), IVIS in vitro optical image (λ ext: 535,605nm and λ emis: 700nm) (b).

Cytocompatibility of PLM comacromer and degradation of PLM-HG hydrogel. Micrograph showing the cell viability of the PLM having different concentrations (500–6000μg/ml) (a), degradation profile of the PLM-HG (b). Data represents Mean±standard error (n =3).

Fluorescent microscope images of PLM-NG nanogels after cellular uptake. L929 cells, when observed with FITC filter and with Texas Red filter (a), with Hela cells (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent PEG based comacromers as excitation dependent fluorophores for biomedical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology, Polymer Science Division, BMT Wing, Thiruvananthapuram 695012, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excitation dependent fluorescence (EDF) characteristics of PLM-NG.

We report a novel multi-modal biodegradable photoluminescent comacromer [poly(propylene fumarate)-PEG-glycine] (PLM) having excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDF) for biomedical applications. The photoluminescence of the synthesized PLM in aqueous and solid state condition, fluorescence life time and photo stability were evaluated. Hydrogels and nanogels were prepared from the PLM by cross linking with acrylic acid. Nanogels exhibited spherical morphology with a particle size of 100nm as evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In vitro cytotoxic and hemolytic studies revealed cytocompatibility. Furthermore, cellular imaging of nanogels on L929 fibroblast and Hela cell lines revealed EDF characteristics. We hypothesize that the EDF characteristics of the synthesized PLM may be attributed to the presence of n–π* interactions of the hydroxyl oxygen atoms of PEG with carbonyl groups of the ester linkages. Taken together, our results indicate that the synthesized PEG-based comacromer can serve as biocompatible fluorophores for various biomedical applications. More importantly, the facile way of synthesizing fluorescent polymers based on PEG with EDF characteristics demonstrated in this work can pave the way for developing more novel biocompatible fluorophores with wide range of biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent biomaterials have found wide application, such as drug delivery, biosensing and tissue engineering [1]. Monitoring of biological activities including distribution of biomolecules in organelles, cells or tissues has been widely recognized as an essential event that determines functional performance [2]. The properties of fluorophores, such as photo stability and quantum yield, influence the detection limit, sensitivity and reliability of the method used [3]. Even though conventional fluorophores like, quantum dots (QDs), organic dyes, and metal nanoparticles have excellent fluorescent properties, they suffer from many serious drawbacks including toxicity, high cost, low sensitivity and non biodegradability [4]. QDs also suffer from intermittence in light emission and instability of quantum yield in different media. To address the toxic effects of QDs, biosynthesis of cadmium selenide (CdSe) quantum dots in Escherichia coli has been recently reported [5].

Near infrared (NIR) emitting fluorophores are promising candidates for bioimaging applications due to deep tissue penetration and minimal tissue auto fluorescence [6]. Common NIR emitting fluorophore dyes include borondipyrromethene (BODIPY), squararine and cyanine. For example, indocyanine green (ICG) has been approved by the FDA for direct intravenous administration in medical NIR imaging of hepatic clearance, cardiac function and retinal angiography. Fluorescein is also approved by FDA primarily for ophthalmology. However, these fluorophores have poor stability after intracellular localization [7,8], require high doses and are associated with low quantum yield and poor photo stability in vivo. Aggregation of ICG in water and binding to serum proteins has been commonly observed with intravenous administration of ICG in humans [9].

Theranostic nanomedicine is another important area in biomedical research where fluorescent materials are widely used. Among the different theranostics agents, polymer-based theranostic agents are preferred as they have great diversity in terms of compositional, structural and morphological characteristics as well as diagnostic and targeting modalities [10,11]. The current strategy of constructing a polymer-based theranostic agent relies on the incorporation of fluorophores that have inherent drawbacks. Hence, photoluminescent polymers having innate IR emission capability and biocompatibility could serve as an ideal theranostic fluorophore.

Excitation dependent photoluminescence (EDF) is an interesting phenomenon exhibited by carbon based nanoparticles which show color tunable photoluminescent properties with different excitation and emission ranges in the visible region. Carbon based nanoparticles, such as graphene [12], graphite [13], carbon nanotubes [14], and pyromellitic diimide nanowire [15], have been reported with EDF phenomenon. These nanomaterials endowed with EDF characteristics, show a unique property of shift in emission wavelength towards longer wavelength region with increase in excitation wavelength. Some of the important mechanisms suggested for this intriguing phenomenon are incomplete solvation of the excited state [16], size distribution of the nanocrystallites [17] and level splitting by doped ions [18]. Although many mechanisms have been proposed, the phenomenon of EDF is still unclear. The reports on EDF phenomena in polymers are abysmal except for one reported on photoluminescent citric acid based polyesters [19].

Polypropylene fumarate (PPF) is unsaturated polyester which is widely used for bone tissue engineering applications. Our group has reported different composites of PPF, such as PPF/phloroglucinoltriglycidyl methacrylate [20], PPF/hydroxyapatite [21], PPF/alginate [22] for orthopedic treatments and cardiac tissue engineering applications. The cross linkable double bonds on PPF favors the formation of hydrogels and nanogels that can be used for different biomedical applications. PEG is a FDA approved polyol which imparts useful properties to materials, such as protein resistance, low toxicity and immunogenicity [23]. Glycine is an essential amino acid which has also been used for synthesizing biocompatible polymer derivatives [24]. In the present study, we have synthesized an aliphatic photoluminescent comacromer (PLM) with EDF characteristics by one pot condensation reaction of PPF, PEG and glycine. The crosslinkable double bonds on PPF facilitated the formation of nanogels with acrylic acid as crosslinker. The prepared nanogels from PLM also exhibited EDF characteristics which indicate that these PEG based PLM could serve as biocompatible fluorophores for different biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Maleic anhydride, maleic acid, 1,2-propylene glycol, polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW: 300), acetone and methanol were purchased from Merck (India). Ammonium persulfate (APS), tetra methyl ethylene diamine (TEMED) and glycine were obtained from Sigma (Bangalore, India). Dialysis bag (500D molecular weight cut-off), Dulbecco’s modified eagle’s medium (DMEM) and Fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Invitrogen (India). MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl thiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay kit was purchased from Hi Media (India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of photoluminescent PPF-PEG-glycine comacromer (PLM)
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescent comacromer (PLM) was synthesized using carboxy-teminated polypropylene fumarate (CT-PPF), PEG and glycine. CT-PPF was synthesized according to our previously published protocols [25]. Briefly, maleic anhydride (1M) and 1,2-propyleneglycol (0.5M) were condensed with morpholine and sodium acetate as catalyst to produce CT-PPF. Afterwards, 0.05M CT-PPF (molecular weight 1389D) and 0.1M PEG (molecular weight 300D) were refluxed under nitrogen atmosphere for 10min at 180°C. Once the constituents in the reaction mixture were melted 0.05M glycine was added and the reaction was continued for another 100min. The comacromer formed was a brownish yellow colored resin. The comacromer was then subjected to dialysis through a dialysis membrane (500D) against deionised water for 3 days to completely remove the unreacted monomers to get the purified comacromer. The comacromer is coded as PLM. The PLM was lyophilized to get the purified PLM microparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of comacromer analogues
ce:section.ce:para: The analogue of PLM coded as PEG-G was synthesized using PEG and glycine. Briefly, 0.1M PEG (molecular weight 300D) was melt condensed with 0.05M glycine under nitrogen atmosphere for 2h at 180°C. Two more analogues of PLM coded as PEG-M and PEG-P were also synthesized by replacing glycine in PEG-G with maleic acid and CT-PPF, respectively. The analogues were then subjected to dialysis through a dialysis membrane (500D) against deionised water for 3 days to completely remove the unreacted monomers.

The comacromer was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectral analyses. FT-IR spectra were obtained in the region of 400–4000cm−1 using a spectrometer (Jasco, FT/IR-4200, Easton, USA), with resin smear placed between KBr plates. Data acquisition and analysis was performed using JASCO’s proprietary Spectra Manager TM II cross-platform software. The nuclear magnetic resonance spectrum of the PLM was recorded using a Bruker 500MHz spectrometer (Bruker Biospin, Billerica, MA) with DMSO and CDCl3 as solvents. The molecular weight of comacromer was determined using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Waters) equipped with a 600 series pump, refractive index detector (Waters 2414) and styragel columns (HR-5E/4E/2/0.5) connected in series. Briefly, 20μl of 1% polymer solution in THF was injected into the system with the mobile phase (THF) set at a flow rate of 1ml/min. Polystyrene standards (Mp: 100,000, 9130 and 162) were used for relative calibration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hydrogel and nanogel from PLM
ce:section.ce:para: A hydrogel was prepared using PLM. Briefly, 0.001M of PLM and 0.0014M of acrylic acid were added to a well of a 48-well plate and gently mixed to get a homogenous paste. The content was further mixed with equal volumes of redox initiators, APS (50μl, 0.5M) and TEMED (50μl, 0.5M) to allow the crosslinking. After 15min, the crosslinked material was scooped out using a clean spatula and washed with distilled water (1×50ml). The hydrogel was coded as PLM-HG. A nanogel was also prepared from PLM. Briefly, 0.8% (w/v) of PLM was dissolved in 5ml water to form a uniform homogenous solution. To this solution, 0.05% (v/v), of acrylic acid was added followed by 1×10−3 M of ammonium per sulfate and TEMED. The beaker containing the above solution was then sonicated for 30min. After sonication a slight turbidity was observed indicating the crosslinking into nanogel. The nanogel was coded as PLM-NG. The morphology of the PLM-NG was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM12 STEM, Netherlands). For TEM analysis, the PLM-NG was ultrasonically dispersed in water to form dilute suspension. Then, one drop was deposited on the carbon-coated copper grid and allowed for 2min after which the excess fluid was removed with a filter paper. The grid was dried for 2 days at room temperature and then examined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of photoluminescent properties of comacromer and nanogel
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence of the PLM comacromer under aqueous condition was studied using CARY-40 spectrofluorimeter at room temperature. PLM solutions (1mg/ml) were loaded inside a quartz cuvette having a path length of 10mm. Similarly 1mg/ml of PEG-P, PEG-M and PEG-G solutions were subjected to photoluminescent studies. The excitation and emission slit width were kept at 1.5nm. The photostability of the PLM comacromer was evaluated under aqueous condition by continuously exciting the PLM at the excitation wavelength of 420nm in a spectrofluorimeter and monitored the change in emission intensity at 525nm. The solid state photoluminescence spectra of the lyophilized PLM microparticle and PLM-HG were recorded using Fluorolog jobin yvon spectrofluorimeter. The time resolved fluorescence experiments were performed using an IBH nanosecond single-photon counting employing a 440nm Nano-LED excitation source and a Hamamatsu C4878-02 micro channel plate (MCP) detector.

The NIR emission properties of the PLM-NG were evaluated using Xenogen IVIS optical imaging system. Briefly 100μl of nanogel (2mg/ml) was loaded on to a 96 well plate and placed inside the optical imaging system. Emission at 700nm was monitored using two different excitation wavelengths, 535nm and 605nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on degradation of the PLM-based hydrogel (PLM-HG)
ce:section.ce:para:
The PLM-HG hydrogel was immersed in PBS solution (pH 7.4) and subjected to agitation in an orbital shaker at 200rpm. The hydrogel samples were removed every 2 days and lyophilized. The change in weight of lyophilized hydrogels was measured. The mass loss of hydrogel was calculated using the equation
Mass loss (%) =
Initial mass − mass after ageing
Initial mass
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity of PLM comacromer
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of PLM was evaluated using L929 fibroblast cell line. Briefly, cells were seeded at a density of 1×105 cells/well (200μl, 96-well tissue culture plate) and cultured in complete DMEM medium at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24h. Then the cells were incubated with different concentrations of comacromer (500–6000μg/ml). After 48h, stock MTT solution (20μl, 5mg/ml, PBS) was added and the plate was incubated for another 4h in a shaker for 15min at 200rpm to dissolve the formazan crystals formed. The absorbance at 570nm was determined using a UV–vis micro plate reader (Tecan). The percentage of viable cells was calculated using the equation given below:
Cell viability =
OD sample
OD control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemolysis test
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro hemolytic potential of PLM-NG was investigated according to ISO 10993-4:2002 standards. Blood from the human volunteer was collected into the anticoagulant, CPD-A. The test sample (1ml) was taken into the three wells of six well polystyrene dish in triplicates and 2ml blood was added. One millilter solution was aspirated immediately for initial analysis and the remaining solution was agitated using an Environ shaker thermo stated at 35±2°C for 30min at 75±5rpm. Four wells of empty polystyrene culture dishes were exposed with blood as reference. The blood samples were centrifuged at 4000rpm for 15min and platelet poor plasma was aspirated. Percentage of hemolysis was calculated by measuring the total hemoglobin using automatic haematology analyser (Sysmex-K 4500). The free hemoglobin liberated in to the plasma before and after exposure to blood was measured in each sample using diode array spectrophotometer. Hemolysis (%) was calculated using the formula.
Hemolysis (%) =
Free Hb
Total Hb/1000
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cellular uptake of the PLM-NG
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular uptake of the nanogel was evaluated by using L929 fibroblast and Hela cell line. Briefly, the cells were seeded at a density of 1×105 cells/well (200μl, 96-well tissue culture plate) and cultured in complete DMEM medium at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24h. The medium was removed and the cells were incubated with nanogels (2mg/ml) in PBS for 4h at 37°C. The cells were then washed with PBS twice and observed under a fluorescent microscope (optica, ITALY) at a magnification of 20× and 40×.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: All experimental data were analyzed by Student’s t tests to evaluate the significance of differences between the sub groups considered. P values <0.05 were statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Preparation and characterisation of photoluminescent comacromer (PLM)
ce:section.ce:para: PLM was synthesized by condensation of carboxy terminated polypropylene fumarate (CT-PPF) with PEG and glycine as described in Scheme 1 . The structure of PLM was confirmed by FT-IR, 1H NMR and 13C NMR analysis. FT-IR spectral analysis of CT-PPF and PLM shows the different functional groups present in both the polymers. The IR spectrum of CT-PPF (Fig. 1 a) reveal peaks at 2985cm−1 (CH stretching vibrations of CHCH bonds of CT-PPF), 1707cm−1 (CO stretching vibrations of ester bonds of CT-PPF), 1643cm−1 (CC stretching of CHCH of CT-PPF), 1382cm−1 (CH bending vibrations of CT-PPF), 1256 (CO stretching of OH and ester linkages of CT-PPF) and 976cm−1 (CH bending of trans CHCH bond of CT-PPF). FT-IR spectra of PLM (Fig. 1b) exhibited additional peaks at 3339cm−1 (OH stretching vibrations of PEG), 2877cm−1 (CH stretching vibrations of CH2 groups PEG) and 1036cm−1 (CN stretching vibrations of glycine) confirming the successful formation of PLM. Furthermore the remaining bands of CTPPF were observed at 1714cm−1 (ester bonds of CT-PPF), 1643cm−1 (CC stretching of CHCH bond of CT-PPF), 1390cm−1 (CH bending vibrations of CT-PPF), 1256cm−1, (CO stretching of OH and ester linkages of CT-PPF), and 976cm−1 (CH bending of trans CHCH bond of CT-PPF). Thus, IR spectral analysis clearly suggests the successful combination of CTPPF, PEG and glycine to form PLM. The structure of the synthesized PLM was further evaluated using NMR technique. Proton NMR spectra of the PLM (Fig. 2 a) shows peaks at 6.8ppm (fumarate double bonds CHCH of polypropylene fumarate), 4.3ppm (CH2 groups of PEG), 3.4ppm (CH2NH from glycine) which proves the formation of PLM. 13C NMR spectra of the PLM (Fig. 2b) also substantiated the structure of the synthesized PLM. The molecular weight of CTPPF is Mn 820, Mw 1389 and polydispersity index 1.69. The molecular weight of PLM is Mn 1225, Mw 3071 and polydispersity index 2.60. The molecular weight analysis of PLM reveals the macromeric nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of photoluminescent behaviour of the PLM comacromer
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescent behavior of PLM was investigated under aqueous condition. PLM exhibited photoluminescent properties with different range of excitations and emissions. When PLM was excited in the visible range from 380 to 540nm, multiple emission peaks were appeared in the spectrum ranging from 480 to 590nm (Fig. 3 a). PLM was exhibiting EDF characteristics which were similar to carbon dots, one of the major fluorescent probes for biomedical applications [26]. The observed excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence of PLM was comparable with that of recently reported natural carbon dots derived from humic substance having different excitation (300–560nm) and emission (420–580nm) wavelengths [27]. The excitation (265–510nm) dependent fluorescent emission (295–678nm) has been also reported with citric acid–glycine based photoluminescent polyester in which citric acid is considered as a contributing component for the photoluminescent behavior [19].

Photostability and photoluminescence life time are two critical parameters which determines the efficiency of any fluorophores for imaging applications. Lack of photostability is one of the major disadvantages with indocyanine green [28]. PLM exhibited photostability without photo bleaching (Fig. 3b), which shows the advantage of this new class of material when compared with organic dyes. Photoluminescence life time data of PLM (Fig. 4 a) reveals diexponential decay path ways with 7 nanoseconds for major components which is higher in comparison with that of most of the endogenous fluorophores [except phenylalanine (7ns) and protophorphyrin IX (up to 15ns)] which exhibit autofluorescence in tissues [29]. The excitation–emission spectra of PLM reveals stokes shift of around 120nm (Fig. 4b), which shows that the stokes shift value of PLM was considerably higher than that of polymer embedded organic dyes like perylene having stokes shift of 50nm [30].

The size of the solid particles of PLM is in micrometer range as observed with a transmission electron microscope (Fig. 5 a). These micro particles exhibited bright green and red emissions (Fig. 5b) under a fluorescent microscope. Hence, photoluminescent behaviors of PLM microparticles were further investigated in the solid state to demonstrate the presence of near IR emissions. PLM microparticles exhibited EDF characteristics in the near IR region i.e., it showed emission at 825nm while exciting in the range of 730–770nm (Fig. 5c). These results show that the synthesized PLM have useful spectral properties such as EDF characteristics under aqueous and solid conditions, higher photo stability and higher fluorescent lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on mechanism of EDF behaviour of PLM comacromer
ce:section.ce:para: Further we have investigated the mechanism of EDF behavior of PLM. The fluorescent life time of PLM is due to diexponential decay pathway, which suggests the presence of two irradiative species in PLM. Hence, to unravel the photoluminescence behavior of the PLM, the photoluminescent property of the analogues of PLM viz PEG-P and PEG-G was investigated. Interestingly, both the PEG-P and PEG-G exhibited photoluminescent characteristics. PEG-G (Fig. 6 a) excited from 380 to 500nm had corresponding emissions between 430 and 560nm. Similarly, PEG-P also exhibited photoluminescent activity (Fig. 6b).

It is worth noting that PLM comprises of condensation products between CTPPF and PEG, and PEG and glycine. Since we have observed fluorescent behavior with the PLM analogues, PEG-P and PEG-G, we conclude that the observed EDF behavior of the PLM system was due to synergistic effect of both of these analogues. UV absorption spectral analysis of PEG-P and PEG-G proves this observation (Fig. 6c). As fluorescence activity was observed with the condensation product of PEG and CTPPF (PEG-P), we further investigated the fluorescence activity for the condensation product between PEG and maleic acid (PEG-M). Surprisingly, PEG-M also exhibited fluorescent characteristics (Fig. 6d). PEG-M, excited in the range of 380–500nm, generated corresponding emissions between 470 and 560nm. Intriguingly, both PEG-M and PEG-G exhibited EDF characteristics with PEG as common unit having multiple carbonyl groups. The observed fluorescence characteristics of both PEG-M and PEG-G were comparable to that of polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized carbon dots having different excitations (340–500nm) and emissions (450–550nm) formed by one step microwave assisted pyrolysis method [31]. Polyesters, in general, contain multiple number of carbonyl groups in their backbone which causes several electronic transitions like n–π*, where n denotes the excited electrons of the unshared electron pairs available on the oxygen and π* denotes the excited electron which goes to the anti-bonding orbital of oxygen–carbon double bond of carbonyl groups. Even though polyesters comprise of several electronic transitions, fluorescence phenomenon is not often associated with these transitions. Unlike general polyesters, PLM has exhibited a unique excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDF) phenomenon. Environment surrounding any chromophore may influence its fluorescent properties. Therefore, polyethylene glycol units that are connected to the carbonyl groups of PLM may influence the emissive properties of carbonyl chromophores inside the PLM. It was reported that electrophilic carbonyl groups undergo a stabilizing interaction with nucleophilic groups, such as oxygen known as n–π* interactions or Buergi–Dunitz interaction, these interactions can influence the photo physical properties of chromophores [32]. These n–π* interactions involve delocalization of lone pair electron (n) of the nuleophilic donor to the acceptor carbonyl groups anti-bonding orbital (π*). In macromolecules like proteins, such n–π* interactions or Buergi–Dunitz interactions are reported [33]. Here in the case of PLM, multiple number of nucleophilic oxygen atoms of PEG can interact with the electrophilic ester carbonyl groups (O⋯CO interactions). Since PLM contains multiple number of OCO bonds, number of n–π* interactions is numerous for the present material which may confer the material the unique property of EDF. Similar EDF phenomenon was reported with monoacylglycerol cluster chromophores in which OH⋯CO (n–π*) interactions between the ester carbonyl groups and protons of the alcohol were responsible factor for exhibiting EDF phenomenon [34]. Since PEG is a FDA approved polyol for various biomedical applications, the EDF characteristics of PEG based polyesters have great scope for the development of new biocompatible fluorescent systems. Polymer–drug conjugates (PDC) are now widely being used for drug delivery applications [35]. In PDC systems, usually a hydrophilic polymer is conjugated with a hydrophobic anticancer drug to increase the solubility and bioavailability of the drug. Hence, if we conjugate a hydrophobic anticancer drug to any of the water soluble PEGylated polymer having EDF characteristics, it may execute both drug delivery and imaging functions within a single domain which is beneficial for cancer therapy. It is worthy to mention that the reports on PEGylated fluorescent polymer with EDF characteristics as a theranostic material are abysmal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EDF behavior of PLM based hydrogel and nanogel (PLM-HG and PLM-NG)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of the PLM hydrogel (PLM-HG) was also confirmed with the Geldoc and solid state photoluminescence spectra. The appearance of PLM-HG hydrogel under normal light and the Gel doc fluorescent image are given in Fig. 7 a. PLM-HG exhibited NIR emission when excited at different regions (510–610nm) (Figm 7b). While comparing the fluorescence spectra of solid PLM micro particle and PLM-HG, PLM-HG exhibited strong emission at 700nm along with a weak emission at 825nm which was observed for the PLM micro particle. Photoluminescence data clearly shows that the PLM-HG hydrogel demonstrated EDF characteristics. These fluorescent hydrogels have tremendous potential for different biomedical applications, such as drug delivery and tissue engineering. When it is used for tissue engineering applications, scaffold degradation can be monitored noninvasively through fluorescence imaging.

The nanogel PLM-NG had a smooth spherical geometry with particle size around 100nm (Fig. 8 a). It has been reported that spherical nanoparticles induce higher cellular uptake with minimal cell membrane disruption [36]. In vitro fluorescent images of PLM-NG were recorded using IVIS (Fig. 8b). The present PLM-NG showed NIR emission (700nm) at two different excitations (535nm and 605nm) without any significant decrease in fluorescence clearly demonstrating an EDF phenomenon. Moreover, due to the large difference in excitation and emission wavelength (>140nm) shown by the PLM-NG, the problem of tissue auto fluorescence can be reduced significantly during bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytocompatibility and hemolysis of PLM and PLM-NG
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity is a major drawback associated with current fluorescent agents used for biomedical applications. Hence, the cytocompatability of PLM comacromer was evaluated using MTT assay for 48h. PLM exhibited good cell viability over a different range of concentrations (Fig. 9 a), revealing cytocompatibility. PLM-HG undergoes degradation within 2 weeks (Fig. 9b) exhibiting biodegradability without any toxic residues. Furthermore, hemolysis is another major challenge for blood contact devices. Our results show that the hemolytic percentage of PLM-NG was only 0.12% even for a high concentration of 1mg/ml which proves hemocompatibility of this material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cellular imaging of PLM-NG
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular imaging capability of the PLM-NG was evaluated using L929 fibroblast and Hela cell lines. The cellular uptake images of PLM-NG in L929 cell (Fig. 10 a) demonstrated dual emissions (green and red) at excitation wavelengths 488nm (green) and 561nm (red), respectively. Similar observations were also noted for Hela cell line (Fig. 10b). The observed multi color illuminations of Hela cells with the PLM-NG were comparable with that of carbon dots having EDF emissions [37]. The observed total intracellular distribution of PLM-NG was consistent with that of polyacrylic acid based nanogels which are believed to overcome cellular barriers and enter intracellular regions due to optimal size and bending of the plasma membrane. [38]. Taken together, these results show that the fluorescence properties of PLM-NG can be exploited for anticancer applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent PEG-based comacromer with innate EDF was designed and synthesized. PLM exhibited appreciable fluorescent life time, good photostability and NIR emissions. The in vitro cytotoxicity and hemolysis studies revealed biocompatibility of this material. Cellular uptake studies on L929 and Hela cells demonstrated the utility of the EDF characteristics of PLM-NG for cancer cell imaging. The ease of fabrication of PLM into bulk hydrogels or nanogels forms demonstrates its versatility for numerous biomedical applications such as drug delivery, cell labeling and biosensing. Furthermore, nanogels are ideal carriers for different types of drugs and proteins, and hence, PLM-NG can become a potent candidate for executing both drug delivery and imaging functions (theranostics) within a single domain. Cumulatively, our data indicates that the synthesized PEG-based comacromer is a promising fluorescent material for different biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Director and Head, BMT Wing, SCTIMST for providing the facilities to carry out this work. The authors acknowledge the help provided by Dr. Shivaram Selvam, Mr. G.T. Finosh, Dr. George Thomas and Dr. Lekshmi Varma during the studies. V.M.V acknowledges the financial support from SCTIMST for Junior Research Fellowship.
